items.title: Notes on Egypt
items.textEn: Source . When my driver picked me up to take me to the New Capital, he was certain there had been a booking mistake. "Are you sure you want to go this way? I can take you to the pyramids or Alexandria instead." There was no mistake. But driving there, past a mural of Egypt’s President Sisi every few hundred meters (sunglasses on, pointing at renderings of his new mega-city) I didn’t know what to expect. There’s only so much you can understand about the New Capital from reading about it online. Almost all the pictures are airbrushed or shot to show only the skyline without the heaps of rubble just to the side. Even the name is confusing. After years of competitions, jury deliberations, and proposed names like "Kemet," "Wedian," and "Memphis," the city is still technically just the ‘New Administrative Capital.’ ​ Cairo isn’t sinking like Jakarta, and nobody seems to know for certain why the New Capital exists. The official reason is to relieve congestion in a city whose metropolitan population is ~20 million. Yes, parts of Cairo are incredibly dense and congested, but there are also parts that are quite nice and spacious. The New Capital isn’t just a convenient annex to Cairo’s outskirts. It’s in the middle of the desert and took us an hour to get there and, according to my driver, "two hours, if there’s traffic." On the night of Eid al-Fitr, Cairo was filled with swarms of teenage boys eager to practice their English with lost-looking foreigners. So I asked them why. Subscribe now Based on my highly formal street survey, the most common response from teenagers was that "Sisi wants to show he’s got big balls." Someone told me he wanted to be like Khufu, who built the first and biggest of the Giza pyramids. A lady who worked at one of the many lingerie shops in Cairo told me, with a look of historical resignation, "Egyptians have always liked to spend our money building great big things in the middle of nowhere." A surprisingly common response was that Sisi built the capital far away and with wide boulevards to make it revolution-proof. In a dense city like Cairo, crowds can surround a government building, but in a capital of eight-lane mega-highways, you’d need a car just to stage a protest. Several people also noted that it’s a place for the rich to escape to. This is expressed most clearly in Madinaty, the gated suburb near the New Capital that, driving through it, makes you question whether you are still in Egypt or in the suburbs of Southern California. Madinaty, a suburb 45 minutes from Cairo for for Egypt’s elite. The photo doesn’t show that it’s surrounded by desert. Source . "It’s okay," said a man in a dusted suit. "China is building it for us." Entering the New Capital [Dune theme song plays] The entrance to the New Capital is a gigantic gate rising up from miles of sand in all directions. Everywhere you go in the New Capital can basically be summarized the same way: the biggest, most gaudy thing you’ve ever heard of, surrounded by sand and rubble. Entrance gate. Source . "That is the largest mosque in Africa," my driver told me, gesturing with his cigarette at a pair of Redwood-like Minarets rising out of the sand. When I asked to stop for a photo, he didn’t take the next off-ramp or even pull over to the shoulder. He simply came to a full stop in the middle of the highway while I got out and took my picture. The mosque was empty. Largest mosque in Africa. Source . We also passed: Sports City, built to anchor Egypt’s Olympic and World Cup bids, housing more than 22 facilities, including a stadium with nearly 94,000 seats, the largest in Egypt and the second largest on the continent. Sports City. Source . Knowledge City , a $950 million tech campus, the government bills as a node in a future digital economy (reminiscent of Zhongguancun in Beijing). Knowledge City. Source . Sisi’s new presidential palace , an enormous disc-shaped structure, said to be shaped in the style of the ancient Egyptian sun god. Presidential palace. Source . An endless row of government ministry offices , each in its own monstrously large and gold-laced building, some with titles so specialized it was hard to imagine the staff filling a single floor, let alone these giant structures. Green River , the capital’s park with fields of glowing green grass running through the desertous landscape, SIX times the size of Central Park. Green River concept rendering. Source . What thematically unifies these monuments is the windswept sand that coats them all, aging the white facades as if the paint has not fully dried. Rubble and half-finished construction stretch in every direction, and stray dogs sleep in the middle of the highways. My movie comp: Bladerunner 2049 . This is no more true than in the downtown, with its cluster of towers, including the tallest building in Africa, built by the Chinese. Tallest building in Africa. China The city was originally supposed to be built by Capital City Partners, a private real estate firm led by an Emirati businessman, but that arrangement collapsed in 2015. Egypt then signed a new agreement with China State Construction Engineering Corporation (CSCEC) to build and finance the administrative core, though CSCEC ultimately narrowed its involvement to the central business district. Europeans are involved too. Siemens built much of the power infrastructure, while the monorail is being built by Alstom (UK), though China is the biggest player. Share The New Capital is being marketed as a "smart city," which in practice means a city pre-wired for surveillance infrastructure, data integration, and perhaps autonomous vehicles. I’ve written before about how China’s approach to autonomous vehicles , at home and abroad, is about more than just exporting cars but about exporting an entire infrastructure stack, reshaping the built environment so that the technology fits cleanly into it rather than the other way around. I didn’t spot any Chinese AVs testing in the New Capital (I didn’t see many vehicles generally), but the roads themselves still showed the foundations. Compared to Cairo’s honking chaos, they were wide, clearly marked, and fitted with cameras and sensors at the finished intersections. A peeling welcome sign in downtown. This is increasingly the playbook. Indonesia is building Nusantara on Borneo to replace Jakarta, with the same decongest-the-old-city rationale, heavy Chinese involvement in its smart city and surveillance infrastructure, and the same ghost-town-in-progress atmosphere, now compounded by a new president who has quietly cut the budget in half and moved on. Senegal built Diamniadio , thirty kilometers outside Dakar, with heavy Chinese financing. Malaysia’s Forest City , developed by Chinese firm Country Garden on reclaimed land near Singapore, is now empty after Malaysian political backlash and capital controls killed demand. (It is reported that Forest City was not targeted at local Malaysians but at upper-class Chinese buyers looking to park wealth abroad, for whom the seafront properties were relatively affordable compared to coastal cities like Shanghai.) China, of course, is doing this on its home turf. Xiong’an , announced in 2017 as Xi Jinping’s personal urban legacy project sits 100 kilometers southwest of Beijing, planned to absorb the capital’s non-essential functions and eventually house 5 million people. Seven years on, it doesn’t seem to be going great. To be fair, there are versions of these neo-cities that work out. Astana replaced Almaty as Kazakhstan’s capital in 1997 and is more or less functional, which is presumably why the Egyptian government sought advice from its planners. But the big question is who bears the cost if it doesn’t go well. For Egypt and countries like it, the answer is obvious. For China, it’s more complicated than it might seem. Subscribe now Chinese SOE construction firms get paid to build, not to populate. Their revenue is front-loaded, and whether the city ever fills up is largely the host country’s problem. The Belt and Road Initiative, the Digital Silk Road, all the framework agreements and memoranda of understanding make the most sense not as instruments of grand strategic maneuvering but as the answer to a simpler question: What do you do with the most formidable construction apparatus in human history once you’ve run out of things to build at home ? The risk to China comes mainly when host countries can’t service their debt (which does happen, as Sri Lanka demonstrated , when it handed over a strategically located port to a Chinese state firm on a 99-year lease after defaulting on its loans) but even then, China has often been able to extract concessions, renegotiate on favorable terms, or simply absorb the loss as the cost of keeping its construction sector employed and its political relationships warm. In Egypt’s case, the dynamic is already visible. In 2023, China converted over $9.4 billion in Egyptian debt into developmental projects and investments, acquiring some state assets in the process. If this is truly the Chinese century, build-outs like this are its texture. The grand geoeconomic questions are worth asking, but driving past millions of empty residential units, I found myself returning to a simpler one: will these cities will actually be good for the people who end up living in them? Abundance in the Desert Millions of units already built, sitting completely empty. Imagine snapping your fingers and emptying Manhattan. Source . We spent an hour trying to reach the residential districts. Every street eventually gave way to rubble about a kilometer short of the entrance, with highway signs pointing toward destinations that went nowhere, like this: What I could see from a distance wasn’t horrible. The buildings weren’t crammed together but weren’t too sprawling either (something close to what urban planners might call gentle density ), and there were hints of genuine architectural thought. Cornices, setbacks, a sens…
items.summaryZh: 文章描述了埃及新行政首都的建设现状，包括宏大而空荡的建筑、巨额中国投资以及大规模基础设施，却几乎没有居民入住，提示投资者需关注此类“千城”项目的实际需求和财务风险。
items.translationZh:
标题：关于埃及的笔记

正文：来源。当我的司机来接我去新首都时，他确信预订出现了错误。 “你确定要走这条路吗？我可以带你去金字塔或亚历山大。” 并没有错误。但在驶向那里的途中，经过每隔几百米就能看到一次埃及总统西西的壁画（戴着墨镜，指着他新巨型城市的渲染图），我不知道该期待什么。仅凭在网上阅读很难真正了解新首都。几乎所有的图片都经过美化或只拍摄天际线，旁边的废墟并未出现。连名字都让人困惑。经过多年竞争、评审以及“Kemet、Wedian、Memphis”等提议后，这座城市在技术上仍仅被称为“新行政首都”。 开罗并不像雅加达那样下沉，也没有人确切知道新首都为何存在。官方理由是缓解一个约有2000万人口的都市区的拥堵。是的，开罗的部分地区确实极度密集拥堵，但也有相当宽敞宜人的区域。新首都并非只是开罗郊区的一个便利附属，它位于沙漠中央，驱车过去花了一个小时，司机说如果堵车会是两小时。开斋节之夜，开罗充斥着渴望与看起来迷茫的外国人练英语的少年们。于是我问他们原因。根据我极为正式的街头调查，青少年最常的回答是：“西西想展示自己的‘大志’”。有人说他想效仿建造第一座也是最大金字塔的胡夫。开罗一家内衣店的女士带着历史性的无奈说：“埃及人历来喜欢在荒凉之地花大钱建宏伟的东西”。一个相当常见的说法是西西把首都建得远离市区并配以宽阔大道，以使其“防革命”。在像开罗这样密集的城市，群众可以包围政府大楼，但在拥有八车道超级高速公路的首都，甚至要发动一次抗议也需要汽车。还有人指出，这里是富人逃离的场所。这在新首都附近的封闭式郊区Madinaty表现得尤为明显，驾车穿过它让人怀疑自己是在埃及还是南加州的郊区。Madinaty，距离开罗45分钟的精英郊区。照片并未展示其四周被沙漠环绕的事实。来源。 “没关系，”一个穿着尘沙外套的男子说。 “中国在为我们建造。”

进入新首都【《沙丘》主题曲响起】
新首都的入口是一座巨大的大门，从四面八方的沙丘中拔地而起。无论你在新首都走到哪里，都可以用同一句话概括：最大、最耀眼的建筑，周围是沙子和碎石。入口大门。来源。 “那是非洲最大的清真寺，”我的司机指着两座像红杉般的宣礼塔对我说，当我请求停车拍照时，他既没有拐下下一条匝道，也没有把车拉到路肩，而是直接在高速公路中段停下，我下车拍照。清真寺里空无一人。非洲最大清真寺。来源。我们还路过：体育城，为埃及的奥运和世界杯申办提供支撑，拥有超过22项设施，包括一座近94,000座位的体育场，是埃及最大、非洲第二大的体育场。体育城。来源。知识城，一个价值9.5亿美元的科技园区，政府称其为未来数字经济的节点（让人联想到北京的中关村）。知识城。来源。西西的新总统宫，一座巨大的圆盘形建筑，据称造型取自古埃及的太阳神。总统宫。来源。连绵不绝的政府部门办公楼，每座都是巨型且镀金的建筑，有些专门化的职能让人难以想象有足够员工填满单层，更别说这些庞大的楼宇。绿河，首都的公园，绿色草坪如荧光般铺展在沙漠中，面积是纽约中央公园的六倍。绿河概念图。来源。将这些纪念性建筑统一起来的，是覆盖其表面的风沙，使白色外墙显得像是油漆尚未干透。碎石和半成品建筑在四处蔓延，流浪狗在高速路中间安睡。我的电影对比：<银翼杀手2049>。这在市中心的高楼群同样真实，其中包括非洲最高的大楼，由中国建造。非洲最高大楼。中国。该城市最初由阿联酋商人领衔的私营房地产公司Capital City Partners承建，但该合作于2015年破裂。随后埃及与中国建筑股份有限公司（CSCEC）签署新协议，建造并融资行政核心，虽然CSCEC最终将参与范围缩小到中央商务区。欧洲公司也参与其中。西门子负责大部分电力基础设施，单轨铁路则由英国阿尔斯通（Alstom）建造，但中国仍是最大玩家。新首都被包装为“智慧城市”，实际上意味着预埋监控设施、数据整合以及或许的自动驾驶车辆。我曾写过，中国在自动驾驶车辆方面的做法，不仅是输出汽车，更是输出完整的基础设施体系，重塑建筑环境，使技术能够干净利落地嵌入，而不是相反。我没有在新首城看到任何中国自动驾驶汽车的测试（整体车辆也不多），但道路本身仍显示出基础设施的痕迹。与开罗的喧嚣混乱相比，这里的道路宽阔、标线清晰，交叉口装有摄像头和传感器。市中心的欢迎标语已经剥落。这正是越来越常见的模式。印尼在婆罗洲建设努桑塔拉，以取代雅加达，理由同样是缓解旧城拥堵，且大量中国参与其智慧城和监控基础设施，呈现出同样的“鬼城”氛围；而新任总统又悄悄将预算减半，继续推进。塞内加尔在大开姆附近建造了Diamniadio，亦有大量中国融资。马来西亚的森林城由中国公司碧桂园在新加坡附近的垫高土地上开发，后来因马来政治反弹和资本管制导致需求消失，现空置。（据称森林城的目标并非本地马来人，而是面向想将财富停放在海外的上层中国买家，相比上海等沿海城市，海滨房产更为可负担。）中国当然在本土也在进行同样的实验。雄安新区，于2017年被习近平宣布为个人城市遗产项目，位于北京西南约100公里，计划承接首都非核心功能，最终容纳约500万人口。七年后进展并不乐观。公平地说，确实有些新城项目是成功的。哈萨克斯坦1997年将首都从阿拉木图迁至阿斯塔纳，基本上已具功能，这也可能是埃及政府向其请教规划师的原因。但关键问题是，如果项目失败，谁来承担成本。对埃及及类似国家来说，答案显而易见。对中国则更为复杂。国有企业（SOE）建筑公司获得的报酬是建造，而非入住。它们的收入在前期集中，而城市是否最终填满居民主要是东道国的责任。 “一带一路”倡议、数字丝绸之路以及所有框架协议和谅解备忘录，更多地不是大国战略的工具，而是对一个更简单问题的回答：在人类历史上最强大的建筑机器在国内已无事可做时，你该把它用到哪里？对中国而言，风险主要在于东道国无法偿还债务（这确实会发生，如斯里兰卡在违约后将战略港口以99年租约交给中国国有企业），但即使如此，中国仍常能获得让步、重新谈判有利条件，或仅仅把损失当作保持建筑业就业和政治关系温度的成本。就埃及而言，这一动态已经显现。2023年，中国将超过94亿美元的埃及债务转化为发展项目和投资，并在此过程中获取了一些国有资产。如果这真的是中国的世纪，这类大规模建设就是其纹理。宏观地缘经济问题值得探讨，但当我在数百万空置住宅单元前驶过时，我回归到一个更简单的问题：这些城市真的会对最终居住其中的人们有益吗？

荒漠中的丰饶
数以百万计的单元已经建成，却完全空置。想象一下，只要打个响指，就把曼哈顿清空。来源。我们花了一个小时想进入住宅区。每条街道最终都在约一公里外变成废墟，路标指向的目的地根本不存在，如图所示：

从远处看并不糟糕。建筑并未挤在一起，也不算过于蔓延（可称为温和密度），还有一些真正的建筑思考痕迹。檐口、退线、感...（后文未完）

items.title: Sen. Slotkin: NDAA, AI guardrails, and banning China's cars
items.textEn: of Michigan, who served in OSD Policy and three terms in the House before joining the Senate Armed Services Committee, joins ChinaTalk to break down what got in, what got voted down, and why NDAA markup days are the only two days a year the Senate acts like a functioning institution. Beyond the NDAA, we also discuss… The AI Guardrails Act, the Anthropic debate, and why no one SecWar or AI company should set the rules for the kill chain, Her bill with Bernie Moreno banning Chinese connected vehicles, The Democratic playbook if the party flips a chamber in November, Data ownership, the Midwest’s data center revolt, and why a healthy democracy would be talking about AI every single day. Listen now on your favorite podcast app . NDAA Markup—The Most Fun to Be Had in the Senate Jordan Schneider: Senator Slotkin of Michigan, formerly of OSD Policy and the House of Representatives — welcome to ChinaTalk. Senator Elissa Slotkin: Thanks for having me. Jordan Schneider: Let’s start with the new NDAA we have, sort of. What are you excited and concerned about? Let’s talk in particular about ROBOCOM, the robotic and autonomous systems combatant command that the committee was excited to kick off. Senator Slotkin: Look, the two days we do the NDAA markup in the Senate are typically my best days of the year. They are behind closed doors — which is probably why people act more normal — and there’s real substantive debate on provisions. Most importantly, say a Republican colleague has a provision and I’m like, "Look, I’d be with you if you just changed X, Y, and Z." They send their staff back to change X, Y, and Z, and three hours later we revisit it, it gets a vote, and it gets in. Or two people have similar amendments on different sides of the aisle, and they go compromise over lunch and fix it. That doesn’t happen enough in this place, so I’m always happy to be in NDAA markup. Jordan Schneider: Maybe before we do the specific stuff — is there any structural change that would make more of the building work like SASC does on NDAA markup days? Senator Slotkin: The way you would do it is probably never gonna happen, because it means you kick the cameras out. That’s the only difference — the cameras are not there, so people don’t feel like they need to play to an audience. They aren’t kicking back with their political talking points; they’re actually doing substantive work. And it’s a pretty hard argument to make: "Hey America, we’re going to be less transparent in the Senate." That’s a hard thing to do. I think there’s a million ways to make the Senate more efficient. The Senate was a caterpillar and could one day be a butterfly, but is right now in the disgusting cocoon and doesn’t know what it’s going to be. I have a million ideas on how to reform this institution. But we’re effective in the NDAA because people are not playing to the bleachers. Jordan Schneider: Do people really need those clips, though? Senator Slotkin: I don’t know that people wake up in the morning and say, "I’m so excited for my five minutes in front of an Armed Services hearing today." But those hearings have been televised for over twenty years, and no one’s gonna be the chairman who says, "I’m kicking out the public," right? And I don’t think the public feels like they want less oversight and insight into what the House and Senate are doing. So it’s a tough position. The way to deal with this is a different vision of how we use these committees — how we do work, how we build coalitions around big ideas. You were just talking about the robotics command. That’s a big deal — the idea to pass something that would potentially be a new command — and unfortunately, we don’t start discussing it until we’re really in markup. Our staff are looking at it before then, for sure, but you could imagine different ways of doing these things. I’m open to all of that. But for the reasons I described, I like NDAA markup because people quite literally roll up their sleeves and do real work. Jordan Schneider: There’s a broader, weird information-asymmetry thing here. There’s so much in the NDAA, and as a principal you can only really get your head around a few provisions. How do you manage that principal-agent issue? Senator Slotkin: Like everywhere in Washington, you have staff who are smarter than you and who are immersed in all of these things — who read everything, who go through hundreds of amendments and flag the most important, the most controversial, the most consequential, the sexiest, whatever. And you prep. If you do your homework — and some of my colleagues really do their homework, some of them don’t — if you care about substance, your staff is saying to you, "Hey, we’ve read through the two thousand pages. Here are the nineteen things we think you really need to know that are gonna come up for real substantive debate, and we’re going to make a recommendation on how you vote." We discuss all that before I ever get in that room. There are giant binders full of amendments — it’s a whole process. But no one is the master of every detail. You get smart staff who can appropriately flag the big, big strategic stuff. Jordan Schneider: Great. All right, let’s come back to my first question — though I do want to do an hour on procedural reform. Maybe for next show. Senator Slotkin: You need a life. You need a life. Jordan Schneider: What else is there to live for? Well, I watched the Knicks last night — that was incredible. What pieces of the NDAA are you excited about and concerned about? Senator Slotkin: I don’t like the Knicks, but okay. When you have two thousand pages, there are things you’ve really fought for and care about, and there are things you don’t agree with. The things I was really glad got in there: the AI Guardrails Act — actually putting some guardrails on the Pentagon and its use of AI, particularly in matters of life and death. Banning Chinese cars on all military bases — I’ve been working on this for four years, and that’s for bases here in the United States but also overseas, where Chinese cars are just everywhere. We had important provisions on Selfridge, an Air Force base in Michigan, on PFOS — making sure the military cleans up after itself when it’s contaminated local communities, including in Michigan. Then there were disappointments — things voted down that left me downright shaking my head, including my own provisions banning uniformed military from collecting ballots and voting machines, breaking the chain of custody of votes. That’s illegal anyways, and we should never be spending a dime on it. That got voted down. Also: not sending the uniformed military to voting locations. I’m very concerned about the authoritarian playbook we’ve seen over and over again, including in Hungary a couple months ago. The president has said if his party doesn’t win this election, then it was rigged. I just don’t want them to precipitate a national security threat such that suddenly they have to send uniformed military to the polls for the first time in our history. Never done that. And we genuinely didn’t talk about the war in Iran. It was this massive elephant in the room. We know they’re all talking about $350 billion, $400 billion as the cost of the war right now — and it’s not over. Could we please discuss that? This war isn’t authorized, so are we gonna appropriate money for a war that’s not authorized by Congress? The top line is huge, and they’ve slashed spending on domestic issues. So there were big strategic issues at the big-picture level, and then a lot of things I was glad got in. AI and the Kill Chain Jordan Schneider: Let’s pick up on a few of those themes. AI starting a nuclear war is something where it’s pretty easy to see the consensus. But as you think down from the strategic to the operational and tactical level, there are a lot of really exciting applications. I was just thinking — to what extent could Claude Mythos do your old job at OSD Policy? Would I rather have a chatbot negotiating an Iran peace deal than Witkoff? Maybe! Probably! What makes sense, and what should people be concerned about? Because clearly there’s going to be a real edge from an effectiveness perspective if you can figure out how to use these tools right. Senator Slotkin: There’s already experimentation going on across our economy on AI, and certainly at the Pentagon — there has been for years. What the whole debate with Anthropic brought up is that there shouldn’t be any one Secretary of Defense or any one company deciding the rules of how AI is used in what we would call the kill chain — decisions of life and death. That should be legislated , because I don’t want someone to come in and change the rules, make them too onerous or too lax. So we took a stab at putting some left and right guardrails on the use of AI, and it’s really about keeping a human being as the ultimate decision maker. We all understand that AI is everywhere — even just doing a Google search, you’re using more AI on a regular basis, and so is the military. If it’s using AI to amalgamate health records or collect information about employees in a faster way, okay. That’s different from making final decisions on when and where to deploy a nuclear weapon, or domestic surveillance, or — again — decisions of life and death. AI is here. I don’t think AI is anywhere near ready to take over negotiations for anybody. And the other piece we got into the NDAA was the unbelievably rigorous testing we need before any of this stuff is fielded. That is the difference. In the race to get more and more technology into the Pentagon, we need to take a beat and make sure we’re properly testing — just like we would a new weapon system, just like we would a new part. I would say even more so, because of some of the advanced capabilities AI brings. You …
items.summaryZh: 密歇根州参议员埃莉萨·斯洛特金在接受访谈时谈及本年度国防授权法案（NDAA）标记日的立法流程、AI监管法案以及在军基地禁止中国汽车的意义，指出这些议题对国家安全与政策制定者的决策具有重要影响。
items.translationZh:
标题：参议员斯洛特金：国防授权法案（NDAA）、AI 防护措施以及禁止中国汽车

正文：密歇根州的参议员斯洛特金，曾在国防部办公厅（OSD）政策部门工作，并在众议院任职三届后加入参议院武装服务委员会，加入 ChinaTalk 讨论了哪些内容进入法案、哪些被否决，以及为何 NDAA 的标记日是参议院一年中仅有的两天能像一个运作良好的机构一样行动。除 NDAA 外，我们还讨论了… AI 防护措施法案、Anthropic 辩论，以及为何没有任何国防部长或 AI 公司应该为致命链（kill chain）制定规则，她与伯尼·莫雷诺（Bernie Moreno）共同提出的禁止中国关联车辆的法案，民主党在十一月若翻盘的策略手册，数据所有权，中西部的数据中心反抗，以及为何健康的民主制度应每日讨论 AI。现在就用你喜欢的播客应用收听。

NDAA 标记—参议院最有趣的时光
Jordan Schneider：密歇根州参议员斯洛特金，曾在 OSD 政策部门和众议院工作——欢迎来到 ChinaTalk。
参议员埃莉萨·斯洛特金：谢谢邀请。
Jordan Schneider：让我们先从新 NDAA 开始谈起。你对哪些内容感到兴奋或担忧？特别谈谈 ROBOCOM——机器人与自主系统作战指挥部，这是委员会期待启动的项目。
参议员斯洛特金：看，参议院进行 NDAA 标记的两天通常是我一年中最好的日子。它们在闭门情况下进行——这可能是人们行为更正常的原因——而且对条款进行实质性辩论。最重要的是，如果一位共和党同僚提出一个条款，我会说：“如果你把 X、Y、Z 改一下，我可以支持”。他们的工作人员会去修改 X、Y、Z，三小时后我们再次审议，投票通过并写入法案。或者两位议员在不同党派有相似的修正案，他们在午餐时达成妥协并加以修正。这在这里并不常见，所以我总是乐于参与 NDAA 标记。
Jordan Schneider：在具体事项之前——是否有结构性变革可以让更多工作像 NDAA 标记日那样在 SASC（参议院武装服务委员会）进行？
参议员斯洛特金：实现方式可能永远不会发生，因为那意味着要把摄像机移走。这是唯一的区别——没有摄像机，人们就不觉得需要向观众表演。他们不再使用政治口号，而是进行实质性工作。这是个很难的论点："美国，我们将减少参议院的透明度"。这很难做到。我认为有成千上万种方法可以提高参议院效率。参议院曾是一只毛毛虫，未来可能会变成蝴蝶，但现在正处在令人不适的蛹中，不清楚会变成什么。我对改革这个机构有无数想法。但在 NDAA 标记中我们有效，因为人们不在为观众表演。
Jordan Schneider：人们真的需要那些摄像头吗？
参议员斯洛特金：我不确定人们早上会不会说，"我对今天在武装服务听证会上的五分钟感到兴奋"。但这些听证会已经电视转播了二十多年，没有人会是那位说"我要把公众踢出"的主席。我也不认为公众想要更少的监督和洞察。我认为解决之道是以不同的方式使用这些委员会——如何工作，如何围绕大想法建立联盟。你刚才提到机器人指挥部，这是件大事——通过可能新设立的指挥部的想法——不幸的是，我们直到真正进入标记阶段才开始讨论。我们的工作人员在此之前肯定已经在研究，但你可以想象还有其他做法。我对所有这些持开放态度。但正如我所描述的，我喜欢 NDAA 标记，因为人们真的卷起袖子，做实事。
Jordan Schneider：这里还有一个更广泛、奇怪的信息不对称问题。NDAA 内容庞大，作为委托人只能抓住少数条款。你如何管理这种委托‑代理问题？
参议员斯洛特金：和华盛顿其他地方一样，你有比你更聪明、沉浸在这些事务中的工作人员——他们阅读所有文件，筛选数百条修正案，标出最重要、最争议、最有影响、最吸引眼球的内容。然后你准备。如果你做功课——有些同事真的做了功课，有些则没有——如果你在乎实质内容，你的工作人员会告诉你："我们已经通读了两千页，这里有十九件我们认为你真的需要了解、会进行实质性辩论的事项，并给出投票建议"。我们在我进入会议室之前就讨论这些。那里有装满修正案的巨型文件夹——这是一个完整的流程。但没有人能掌握每个细节。你得到的是能恰当地标出重大战略事项的聪明工作人员。
Jordan Schneider：很好。回到我的第一个问题——虽然我想花一个小时讨论程序改革，也许下期节目再聊。
参议员斯洛特金：你需要生活。你需要生活。
Jordan Schneider：还有什么值得活的？我昨晚看了尼克斯——太棒了。
参议员斯洛特金：我不喜欢尼克斯，但好吧。当你面对两千页文件时，有些是你一直争取并关心的，有些则不赞同。我真的高兴进入法案的内容包括：AI 防护措施法案——在国防部使用 AI，尤其是涉及生死决定时，设置一些防护措施。禁止在所有军基地使用中国汽车——我为此工作了四年，这不仅适用于美国国内的基地，也适用于海外，那里的中国汽车随处可见。我们在密歇根的谢尔弗里奇空军基地（Selfridge）以及关于全氟辛烷磺酸（PFOS）的重要条款——确保军方在本地社区受到污染后进行清理。还有让人失望的——被否决的条款包括禁止制服军人收集选票和投票机器，破坏投票链的保管。这本身就是非法的，我们不该为此花钱。还有：不让制服军人去投票地点。我非常担心我们在匈牙利等国看到的专制剧本。总统曾说如果他的党派在选举中失利，那就是选举被操纵。我不想他们制造出国家安全威胁，以至于必须第一次让制服军人去投票站——历史上从未有过。我们真的没有讨论伊朗战争。那是房间里巨大的大象。我们知道他们都在谈论 3500 亿、4000 亿美元的战争成本——而且战争还未结束。这场战争未经国会授权，我们还能为未经授权的战争拨款吗？总额庞大，且他们削减了国内支出。所以在大局层面有重要的战略议题，也有许多让我高兴的内容。AI 与致命链（kill chain）
Jordan Schneider：我们来聊聊这些主题。AI 导致核战争的可能性比较容易达成共识。但在从战略到作战、战术层面下降时，有许多令人兴奋的应用。我在想——Claude Mythos 能否取代你在 OSD 政策部门的旧工作？我更愿意让聊天机器人去谈判伊朗和平协议吗？也许！可能！哪些是合理的，哪些值得担忧？因为显然如果你能正确利用这些工具，将在效能上获得真正的优势。
参议员斯洛特金：经济中已经有 AI 的实验，五角大楼也多年如此。Anthropic 辩论提出的关键是，不应让任何一位国防部长或任何一家公司决定 AI 在所谓致命链中的使用规则——生死决策应立法规定，我不想有人随意改变规则，使其过于严格或过于宽松。所以我们试图在 AI 使用上设定左右两端的防护措施，核心是保持人类作为最终决策者。我们都明白 AI 已无处不在——即使是普通的谷歌搜索，你也在使用 AI，军方也是如此。如果它用于汇总健康记录或更快收集员工信息，那可以接受。这与在何时何地部署核武器、国内监视或其他生死决策不同。AI 已经来到这里。我不认为 AI 已经准备好取代任何人的谈判工作。而我们在 NDAA 中加入的另一项是对任何此类系统进行极其严格的测试，这就是区别所在。在把更多技术投入五角大楼的竞赛中，我们需要停下来，确保进行适当的测试——就像对新武器系统进行测试一样，甚至更应该，因为 AI 带来的高级能力。……

items.author: https://www.chinatalk.media/feed
items.title: Paul Kennedy on Great Powers
items.textEn: What a profound honor to have Paul Kennedy on the ChinaTalk podcast. Kennedy is my favorite living historian and the writer who’s most shaped my intellectual development. His analysis underpins what you hear on this show every week. The Rise and Fall of the Great Powers is an epochal work that traces global power transitions from 1500 to the present. It’s gripping, forest-and-trees scholarship at its finest. Equally impressive in different ways is his book, The Rise of Anglo-German Antagonism, 1860 to 1914 . Not only is it god-tier diplomatic history, but it also gives you a feel for the era through its explorations of social, economic, domestic, political, and cultural dimensions of Anglo-German relations. There are fascinating US/China analogies that we’ll get into in this podcast. His two most recent works directly inform the military coverage on ChinaTalk. Engineers of Victory looks at how people and the systems they worked within solved engineering challenges that turned the tide for entire theaters in World War II. His latest, Victory at Sea: Naval Power and the Transformation of Global Order in World War II , is a sweeping history of a radical transformation in the balance of military power, from the mid-1930s when America was just gaining prominence, to after World War II, when it had no other significant naval competitor. The Parliament of Man: A History of the United Nations made me interested in international organizations and gave me my senior thesis topic about the creation of the UN. What Kennedy taught me more than anything is this — sweat the details, look at the individual players, and zoom out often enough to understand what truly shapes the long-term fate of nations. Over the course of this episode, we pick up themes from all across his work: Great Power rivalries of the late 19th-early 20th centuries, the China echoes today, and why potential antagonisms turn nice and others turn belligerent, The persistent struggles of liberal internationalists and why they rarely get the outcomes they want, Why China today is not Germany of the late 19th and early 20th centuries, The surprising ways geography shapes global power dynamics, How fear spreads among nations and why mutual suspicion is so hard to escape, Why top powers blow it and lose their dominant place in the world, How systems and innovation win wars. And much more, including salutary lessons from the Dutch and Swedes on boring yet prosperous futures, how Churchill’s interest in gadgets influenced the course of the Second World War, and why transformative action from the UN remains unlikely in the near future. Listen now on your favorite podcast app . How to Win Friends and Alienate Nations Jordan Schneider: What is national power, and why does it matter? Paul Kennedy: Since the earliest struggles between tribes and later between larger groups of people with their symbols and more organized armies, and in particular, the emergence of the modern nation-state around the year 1500 (as historians roughly date it) — this entity has existed. The nation-state, with its capacity to raise taxes, conscript, and enroll men to fight under the direction of, say, a Dutch republic or a Spanish monarchy, has fundamentally shaped global affairs. Since that era, with the rise of the nation-state in Western Europe and its subsequent adoption worldwide, this thing we call "the country," "the nation," or "the state," has operated within what political scientists term an anarchic international system. "Anarchy" here doesn’t necessarily mean everyone slaughtering everyone else, but simply the absence of a higher authority controlling global affairs. The United Nations General Assembly lists approximately 189 or 192 nation-states, large and small. We give them all a membership, a vote in the General Assembly. Almost all of them have some sort of diplomatic service as well as a domestic home service, have a government and a constitution of one form or another, and interact with the other nation-states, the other players in the system. Because the decisions they take over time impact individuals in different ways, we pay attention to this, to international history. We pay attention because this great power system, in terms of a relatively harmonious relationship between the nation-states, broke down into massive, expensive, and bloody warfare in the First and Second World Wars. It behooves us to understand this great power system and the nation-states which play such a role within the system. Jordan Schneider: I want to begin with a quote from a German politician in 1903, which comes back to your idea of tribes. Eduard Bernstein , in a debate between the left and right in the Reichstag over Anglo-German trade relations said, "The entire question is this: When do we consider the position of one land to another in the manner of jealous, acquisitive tribes, which if one robs, the other is robbed, and when do we consider it from the standpoint of a peaceful exchange of nations?" Paul Kennedy: A nice quote from Bernstein, who was one of the leaders of the left liberal and progressive parties in Imperial Germany, which were somewhat swept away with the coming of war in 1914. But he was still optimistic then. You can tell from his question in that Reichstag debate that he was presenting essentially two large, contradictory, and distinct versions of understanding international affairs. Was international affairs to be regarded as a benign relationship between these advanced states and societies in which you traded with each other? Great Britain in 1903, when Bernstein was speaking, was the number one commercial partner of Imperial Germany. Germany relied heavily upon exporting many of its manufactured goods to the UK market. The UK exported a large amount of imperial and tropical produce to the German market. This harmonious interchange of commercial goods between states, as well as the harmonious interchange of their ideas, university students, travelers, tourists — that’s the benign view of international relations. The more malign, suspicious, or competitive view is that these are rival tribes. They are in some sort of inherent, natural fighting, suspicious relationship with each other. So one is a hostile relationship and the other is benign and friendly. As the years evolved towards 1914, these two interpretations of international affairs were strongly contested because if one won and prevailed, all would be well, benign trade would rise. If the other was true, then you had to keep your weapons sharp. You had to keep your navies big and strong. You had to have intelligence systems gathering information about what the other might be up to. You had to be suspicious rather than trusting. Those are two very different views of world affairs. Jordan Schneider: The answer, of course, is both. Humanity would not have been able to reach the heights it is today if we hadn’t figured out how to cooperate with each other, but looking over the past 5,000 years, there are moments in time when that mindset shifts, particularly when it comes to great powers. You raise this fascinating counterfactual of England not being anxious about America’s rise, even though they have a past history of fighting wars. The "ties that bind" — but there were plenty of those also between Germany and the United Kingdom. Is there a theory about when the mindsets start to shift from "we can cooperate with these guys" to "we can’t really in the end"? Paul Kennedy: This is a question I pose to my students interested in international affairs and why systems break down. In the late 19th century, the number one industrial trading naval power of Great Britain was finding the world less and less comfortable because its relative share was diminishing, and the two quickly rising countries which were diminishing those shares were Imperial Germany on one side and the United States of America on the other side. Yet, as you allude to, the story is that the British turn to an ever-more-friendly, almost contractual relationship with the United States and to an ever-less-friendly, ever-more-suspicious relationship with Imperial Germany. Why should Great Britain, with these relatively sane and intelligent decision-makers in Whitehall, turn against one and turn in favor to the other? You can consider some of the major and obvious reasons and ask students to try to weigh them or evaluate their significance. One is geography — sheer distance. Even if it is a rising great power, if it is 3,000 miles away, that’s less of a problem than if this rising great power is just 15 hours steaming across the North Sea in the middle of the night. If that rising power on the other side of the Atlantic, as it threatened to be back in the 1840s and 1860s, might gobble up your dominion state of Canada, that would be different. But by 1903 or so, American-Canadian relations and disputes over borders and seal fisheries and other things had been resolved. So there was no hostile threat from this distant rising power across the Atlantic. Historians also point out the enormous advantage of a common language . You could speak to each other and feel part of a longstanding English cultural world, despite the fact the United States was a polyglot nation with all sorts of cultural inputs. Around the turn of the century, identification with Anglo culture was predominant among American elites. That helped. A good number of American politicians, from Teddy Roosevelt onward, grew up very much in this Anglo-historical tradition, and on the British side, once the elderly, suspicious Lord Salisbury had passed, there were also quite a few committed Americanophiles. Then there is the issue of ideology — an autocratic nation on one side (Imperial Germany) and a democratic parliamentary or congressional nation on the other. So you can start listing a whole number of reasons why it was relatively easy for the British to see the r…
items.summaryZh: 本文介绍了历史学家保罗·肯尼迪在ChinaTalk播客的访谈，回顾了他关于大国兴衰、英德冲突以及二战军事变革等著作，并探讨这些历史研究如何帮助理解当代中美关系、国际秩序及大国竞争的动因，对投资者把握地缘政治风险具有参考价值。
items.translationZh:
标题：保罗·肯尼迪谈大国

正文：能够在ChinaTalk播客上邀请保罗·肯尼迪，真是莫大的荣幸。肯尼迪是我最喜爱的在世历史学家，也是对我的智识发展影响最大的作者。他的分析是本节目每周内容的基石。《大国的兴衰》是一部划时代的作品，追踪自1500年至今的全球权力转移，内容引人入胜，是宏观与微观研究的典范。另一部同样令人印象深刻的作品是《英德对抗的崛起，1860-1914》，它不仅是顶级的外交史著作，还通过社会、经济、国内、政治和文化层面的探讨，让人感受到那个时代的氛围。书中有许多美国/中国的类比，我们将在本期播客中深入探讨。他最近的两部作品直接影响了ChinaTalk对军事的报道。《胜利的工程师》考察了二战期间人们及其所处系统如何解决工程难题，扭转了整个战区的局势。他最新的《海上胜利：二战中的海军力量与全球秩序的转变》是一部宏大的历史，描绘了从1930年代中期美国刚刚崭露头角，到二战结束后美国成为唯一显著的海军竞争者的军力平衡根本性转变。《人类议会：联合国的历史》让我对国际组织产生兴趣，并成为我关于联合国创建的毕业论文课题。肯尼迪教给我的最重要的理念是——关注细节，观察个体角色，并时常抽离全局，以理解真正塑造国家长期命运的因素。在本期节目中，我们将从他的各部著作中抽取主题：19世纪末至20世纪初的大国竞争、当今中国的回响、为何某些潜在的敌对关系会变得友好而另一些却变得敌对、自由主义国际主义者的持续斗争以及为何他们很少实现预期目标、为何当代中国不同于19末20初的德国、地理如何出人意料地影响全球权力动态、恐惧如何在国家之间蔓延以及为何相互猜疑难以摆脱、为何顶级大国会失误并丧失全球主导地位、系统与创新如何赢得战争，等等。还包括荷兰和瑞典在平凡却繁荣的未来中的启示、丘吉尔对小工具的兴趣如何影响二战进程，以及为何联合国的变革性行动在短期内仍不太可能实现。立即在您喜欢的播客应用中收听。

《如何赢得朋友并疏远国家》
乔丹·施耐德：什么是国家实力，为什么它重要？
保罗·肯尼迪：自最早部落之间的冲突以及后来的更大规模群体之间的冲突——有了符号和更有组织的军队，尤其是约在1500年左右出现的现代民族国家（历史学家大致这么划分）——这种实体一直存在。民族国家具备征税、征兵、并在比如荷兰共和国或西班牙君主制的指挥下组织人们作战的能力，根本塑造了全球事务。自那时起，随着民族国家在西欧的崛起并随后被全球采纳，我们所称的“国家”“民族”或“政体”就在政治学家所谓的无政府国际体系中运作。这里的“无政府”并不一定意味着人人相残，而是指缺乏控制全球事务的更高权威。联合国大会列出大约189或192个国家，大大小小。我们给予它们全部成员资格，均有在大会中的投票权。几乎所有国家都有一定形式的外交服务以及国内行政机构，拥有政府和宪法，并与其他国家——系统中的其他参与者——互动。因为它们的决策随着时间的推移以不同方式影响个人，我们关注这些国际历史。我们关注的原因是，这个大国体系——在相对和谐的国家关系框架下——在第一次和第二次世界大战中崩溃为大规模、昂贵且血腥的战争。了解这个大国体系以及在其中扮演角色的民族国家是有益的。
乔丹·施奈德：我想引用一位德国政治家1903年的话，它呼应了您关于部落的观点。爱德华·贝尔恩斯坦在一次关于英德贸易关系的议会辩论中说：“整个问题在于：我们何时应以嫉妒、掠夺性部落的方式看待一块土地与另一块土地的关系——一方抢劫，另一方亦被抢劫；何时应以民族之间和平交流的立场来考量？”
保罗·肯尼迪：这是贝尔恩斯坦的一句好引语。他是德意志帝国左翼自由进步派的领袖之一，随着1914年战争的爆发，这些势力基本被扫除。但他当时仍保持乐观。从他在议会辩论中的提问可以看出，他基本上提出了两种截然相反、且互相冲突的国际事务理解方式。国际事务是应被视作这些先进国家和社会之间的良性关系——相互贸易？1903年，贝尔恩斯坦发言时，英国是德意志帝国的头号商业伙伴。德国大量制造业产品出口到英国市场；英国则向德国市场出口大量帝国及热带产品。这种国家间商品的和谐交换以及思想、大学生、旅行者、游客的交流——就是国际关系的良性视角。另一种更恶意、更怀疑或更具竞争性的观点则认为这些是竞争的部落，它们之间存在某种固有、自然的斗争与猜疑。因此，一种是敌对关系，另一种是友好和善的关系。随着时间推移至1914年，这两种对国际事务的解释被激烈争论；如果良性观点占上风，一切将顺利，友好贸易上升；若竞争视角占据主导，则必须保持武器锋利，保持海军强大，建立情报系统收集对方动向，保持怀疑而非信任。这两种世界观截然不同。
乔丹·施奈德：答案当然是两者兼而有之。如果没有相互合作，人类不可能达到今天的高度，但回顾过去5000年，特别是在大国崛起的节点，思维方式会发生转变。您提到的英国不担忧美国崛起的假想——尽管英国有战争历史——以及英德之间的“束缚之线”。是否有理论解释何时从“我们可以合作”转向“我们不可共存”？
保罗·肯尼迪：这是我给有志于国际事务的学生们提出的问题，也是系统为何崩溃的核心。19世纪末，英国作为工业、贸易和海军强国，发现其相对份额在不断下降，而两个快速崛起的国家——德国帝国和美利坚合众国——正在蚕食其份额。然而，正如您所言，英国对美国采取了越来越友好、几乎是契约式的关系，而对德国则变得愈发不信任。为何英国内阁的理性决策者会对一方转向，对另一方转向友好？可以考虑一些显而易见的因素并让学生权衡其重要性。其一是地理——纯粹的距离。如果崛起的大国距离3000英里之外，比起仅在北海夜航15小时的邻近对手，威胁要小得多。如果大西洋对岸的崛起大国在1840至1860年代可能吞并您在加拿大的领地，那情形截然不同。但到1903年左右，美加之间的边界争端、海豹渔业等问题已基本解决，远方的崛起力量并未构成直接威胁。历史学家也指出共同语言的巨大优势。尽管美国是多语言国家，但在那个时代美国精英仍深受英国文化影响，英语是共同语言，促进了相互认同。许多美国政治人物——从西奥多·罗斯福开始——在英美历史传统中成长；在英国方面，老龄且多疑的萨尔斯伯里勋爵去世后，亦有不少亲美人士。另一个因素是意识形态——一方是专制的德国帝国，另一方是民主的议会或国会制国家。因此，您可以列出诸多原因，解释为何英国相对容易对美国保持友好，而对德国则更为警惕。

items.title: A Night at the UFC
items.textEn: In December 2015 I slipped off a stage onto my forehead, causing a concussion that kept me on medical leave for a year ( and wrote about it here ). Also in December 2015, Conor McGregor threw a counter left cross to knock out José Aldo in 13 seconds, kicking off my MMA fandom. What followed was a decade of streaming fights. But I had never seen one in person. With the UFC coming to New York and China’s greatest female fighter Zhang Weili headlining, I got my hands on a press pass. On Media Day, I walked into a midtown hotel conference room hoping to generate rooting interests by press conference personality. Expecting WWE trash talk, instead I saw reserved professionalism. They were calm, talked about their young kids and Jesus. An older fighter Beneil Dariush, when asked about his legacy, said "My legacy is Christ!" Dagestani (a Muslim ethnic minority in Russia) champion Islam Machachev was the only one relaxed enough to make jokes, while Carlos Prates, Brazilian fighter famous for smoking half a pack a day, talked some shit about his opponent being washed and fans wanting blood. Zhang Weili, former champion, is a feminist hero in China for pushing back against gender norms around aesthetics and marriage. I asked her a few questions in my extra-bad-because-I-was-nervous Chinese. Jordan: You’ve spoken in the past about how Chinese culture influences your fighting, but MMA is a famously international sport. Can you expand on how your Chinese roots influence your fighting style? And what would you want the fighting world to better understand about China? The translator started summarizing what I said but Zhang Weili cut her off saying ‘I pretty much got it’ (我大概明白了), in so doing saving me a ton of face Zhang Weili: Roots are really important and make me more confident. The most important thing is how it opens up my thinking to integrate more things. Chinese culture is very inclusive, as is MMA culture. Jordan: Sorry, but this question isn’t super professional, but I have a daughter that just turned 1. What do you want to say to the next generation of women? Zhang: Bravely be yourself, as long as you’re the best version of yourself you’ll be great. (video timestamped below) The person I was most hoping to dislike was Zhang Weili’s opponent, longtime champ and boring fighter Valentina Shevchenko. One reporter politely called her out for fighting the same fight for years, asking: "You have been so dominant for so long, and sometimes fans or critics say they want to see a ‘war,’ they want to see you get into a crazy exchange. When you hear that, does it annoy you?" Her response: I hear sometimes people say, ‘Oh, we want to see crazy exchange, we want to see blood.’ But this is not... this is not smart. My goal is always to win and to show perfect technique. You want to show not only fight—like what everyone can do, like [a] street fight or whatever—you want to show the art of martial arts, the beauty of fighting. And this is my mindset. I asked a follow up , hoping to get her to expand on "what does the artistry of martial arts mean to you?" Said Valentina: We now live in a dream of great athletes—to be able to fight a universal martial art that contains all techniques. It’s all together now—the martial art technique that includes karate, tae kwon do, boxing, jiu jitsu, all kinds of wrestling. I’m very happy I’m living at the right time to show the skills I’ve developed over 32 years of practicing. This is the art and beauty of fighting. After that, I couldn’t bring myself to hope Zhang dished her out real brain damage. But did the fight live up to Valentina’s vision? And does anyone in a UFC crowd want artistry over blood? My parents first took me to Madison Square Garden when I was five to see the circus. Fast forward thirty years and I’m walking in the media entrance feeling like I’m somebody. Up three levels, I drop my bag in a cramped media room, where reporters barely watching eat on folding tables MSG-provided sad penne vodka, less sad Italian cookies, and mini water bottles. The press box they sent me to was on the sky bridge. I sat down next to two Chinese reporters for the fights. One was taking video, a recent graduate of Columbia’s architecture MA program who works for my old employer Kuaishou part time, mainly to get into Knicks games for free. 1 A fit Chinese guy and his wife sat next to us. I introduced myself and asked him what outlet they worked for. My new friend, embarrassed for me, told me he was The Leetch , one of China’s biggest MMA stars. "Jordan you really need to take a 功课, do your homework…" My credibility shot, I wandered out of the press box and into the nosebleeds, where CTE poster child Giants RB Cam Skattebo got the biggest cheer as the jumbotron panned across celebrity row. Fights are too exciting and too boring. Minutes-long grappling exchanges require training in jiu jitsu to make any sense of. They’re also illegible from distance, leading the entire arena to stare at the jumbotron. But when the fighters are standing, you are always half a second from a spectacular knockout. That tension commands attention like watching the Olympic hockey 3v3 overtime, but when taken in over six hours of fights it begins to numb. Watching UFC feels oddly more comfortable than the NFL from a concussion perspective. Here concussions are the point, and everyone who signs up for the sport has no illusions about what it will do to their brain. Watching so many knock outs in succession makes it seem like a less big deal once they start moving afterwards. I felt for the concussees less on account of brain damage than twenty thousand fans thrilled at them losing the worst way there is to lose a sport. Take Bo Nickal, who threw a leg kick that left his opponent unresponsive for minutes. Once Bo turned around, he stopped preening and looked genuinely concerned at what he’d done. Said Bo afterwards, "That’s honestly to me one of the worst parts about what we do — having to hurt other people, do damage to people. We’re taking years off each other’s lives. It’s sad, but it’s part of it. For me, it’s the strategy, the discipline, the commitment — that’s why I love this sport, and how difficult it is to be the best in the world. Hurting other people is one of the downsides of this sport. I know people like it, but in my mind it’s not very fun. It’s sad, but it is what we signed up for." In contrast there are a handful of fighters who play up the violence. Said Carlos Prates, famous for his boozing and cigarette habit, "you guys pay to watch violence, to watch blood!" But even he acknowledged after his win, "I don’t really like hitting a guy like that when he falls, but he fell while still conscious, in guard. So I thought, ‘I’m going to have to finish him.’ But that’s it, it happens. I hope he’s okay." Shevchenko spent 25 minutes on top of Zhang Weili, showing that the beauty of fighting sometimes looks a lot like getting sat on. Twenty thousand people booed. Valentina didn’t care. And at least Zhang didn’t get concussed. 2 I hoped to continue ChinaTalk’s UFC coverage next week from the White House, but unfortunately… "They maxed capacity!" apologized the comms person. I really hope Caity Weaver or George Saunders got the slot instead of me. 1 We exchanged WeChats and he saw my very amateur Chinese landscapes. He said he used to paint growing up as well, and the second friend was like "oh is Jordan any good?" He respected me enough not to glaze me… 2 ICE is targeting UFC fans to hire.
items.summaryZh: 文章记录作者首次现场观看UFC赛事的经历，重点描述了与中国拳手张伟丽及其他选手的交流、赛场氛围以及对拳击艺术与伤害的思考，提醒投资者关注体育娱乐行业的文化价值与健康风险。
items.translationZh:
标题：UFC之夜

正文：2015年12月，我从舞台上摔下，撞到前额导致脑震荡，休病假一年（并在此写过相关内容）。同年12月，康纳·麦格雷戈（Conor McGregor）用左手反打十字拳在13秒内击倒约瑟·阿尔多（José Aldo），开启了我对 MMA（综合格斗）的狂热。随后十年，我一直在观看格斗赛事的直播，却从未现场观看过。UFC 把赛事安排在纽约，中国最强女子选手张伟丽（Zhang Weili）担纲主赛，我获得了媒体通行证。媒体日，我走进曼哈顿一家酒店的会议室，原本期待通过新闻发布会的表现来激起观众兴趣，结果却看到一种克制的职业精神。选手们很平和，谈及自己的小孩和耶稣（Jesus）。老将贝内尔·达鲁什（Beneil Dariush）被问及遗产时说：“我的遗产是基督！（My legacy is Christ!）”。达吉斯坦（Dagestan，俄罗斯的穆斯林少数民族）冠军伊斯兰·马恰切夫（Islam Machachev）是唯一放松到开玩笑的选手，而巴西选手卡洛斯·普拉蒂斯（Carlos Prates）以每天抽半包烟闻名，在赛后嘲讽对手被洗脑，粉丝想要血腥场面。前冠军张伟丽在中国因挑战审美和婚姻等性别规范而被视为女权英雄。我用紧张到几乎不堪的中文向她提了几个问题。

Jordan：您过去曾谈及中国文化对您格斗的影响，但 MMA 是一项跨国运动。能否展开说明您的中国根基如何影响您的格斗风格？以及您希望格斗界对中国有何更深的认识？
翻译员开始概括我的提问，但张伟丽打断她说：“我基本明白了”（我大概说清楚了），从而帮我省了很多面子。

张伟丽：根基非常重要，让我更有自信。最关键的是它开启了我的思维，让我能整合更多东西。中国文化很包容，MMA 文化也是如此。

Jordan：抱歉，这个问题不算特别专业，但我有一个刚满一岁的女儿。您想对下一代的女孩说什么？
张伟丽：勇敢做自己，只要你是最好的自己，你就会很棒。（视频已标注时间）

我最期待不喜欢的人是张伟丽的对手——长期冠军且乏味的瓦伦蒂娜·舍甫琴科（Valentina Shevchenko）。一位记者礼貌地问她：“您这么多年一直保持统治地位，有时粉丝或评论家说想看‘战争’，想看您进行疯狂的交锋。当您听到这种要求时会烦吗？”

她回应：有时会有人说‘我们想看疯狂的交锋，想看血’，但这不……这不聪明。我的目标始终是赢，并展示完美的技术。你想展示的不仅是格斗——比如每个人都能在街头格斗中做到的——而是展示武术的艺术，格斗的美感。这就是我的思维方式。

我追问她关于“武术艺术对您意味什么”，她说：我们现在生活在伟大运动员的梦想中——能够使用包含所有技术的通用武术。如今所有技术都融合在一起——包括空手道、跆拳道、拳击、柔术以及各种摔跤。我很高兴自己生在正确的时代，能展示我32年练习所积累的技能。这就是格斗的艺术与美。

之后，我不敢期待张伟丽会把舍甫琴科打得脑损伤。可是比赛是否实现了舍甫琴科的愿景？现场观众真的更看重艺术而非血腥吗？

我父母五岁时第一次带我去麦迪逊广场花园看马戏。三十年后，我走进媒体入口，感觉自己像个重要人物。上了三层，我把包丢进一个拥挤的媒体室，记者们几乎不看现场，只在折叠桌上吃着 MSG 提供的悲惨意面伏特加、稍好一点的意式曲奇和小瓶装水。媒体席在天桥上。我坐在两位中国记者旁边，其中一位正在拍视频，刚从哥伦比亚建筑学院拿到硕士学位，兼职为我以前的雇主快手（Kuaishou）工作，主要是为了免费进尼克斯（Knicks）比赛。

一位健壮的中国男子和妻子坐在我们旁边。我自我介绍并询问他们的媒体背景。我的新朋友因为尴尬而告诉我，他是“The Leetch”，中国最大的 MMA 明星之一。“Jordan，你真的需要多做功课……”我的可信度受挫，我走出媒体箱，来到高位看台，看到作为 CTE（慢性创伤性脑病）典型案例的巨人队跑卫卡姆·斯卡特博（Cam Skattebo）在巨型屏幕上获得最大欢呼。赛事既激动人心又乏味。几分钟的抓握交换需要柔术训练才能看懂；在远处观看几乎无法辨认，导致全场都盯着巨幕。只有当选手站立时，你才会在半秒内看到壮观的击倒。这种紧张感像观看奥运会冰球 3v3 加时赛，但持续六小时的格斗后会让人麻木。相比 NFL，观看 UFC 在脑震荡层面更让人感到舒适，因为这里的脑震荡是比赛的核心，参赛者对其后果毫不掩饰。连续看到多次击倒后，观众对选手受伤的感受似乎变得淡漠，只剩下两万名观众为他们以最糟方式失利而感到刺激。

以波·尼卡尔（Bo Nickal）为例，他踢出一记腿踢致对手数分钟无反应。尼卡尔随后转身，停止炫耀，诚恳地说：“对我而言，这项运动最糟的部分就是——伤害他人，毁掉别人的生命。我们在互相夺走岁月，这很悲哀，但也是我们选择的部分。对我来说，这是一种策略、纪律、投入——这也是我热爱这项运动的原因，想要成为世界最佳的困难所在。伤害他人是这项运动的副作用。我知道有人喜欢，但在我心里并不有趣。它很悲哀，但这就是我们签约时的约定。”

相反，也有少数选手在暴力上做文章。巴西选手卡洛斯·普拉蒂斯（以酗酒和抽烟闻名）说：“你们付钱来看暴力，看血！”但他在获胜后也承认：“我并不喜欢把已经倒地的选手继续打，但他倒地时仍然清醒，处于防守姿态。所以我想‘必须结束他’，就这么回事，发生了。我希望他没事。”

舍甫琴科在张伟丽身上压制了 25 分钟，展示了格斗之美有时看起来更像被压在下面。两万观众嘘声四起，舍甫琴科毫不在意。至少张伟丽没有受到脑震荡。

我本计划下周在白宫继续 ChinaTalk 的 UFC 报道，却因“容量已满”而被告知失约。我真希望凯蒂·韦弗（Caity Weaver）或乔治·桑德斯（George Saunders）能得到那档位。

（注：文中提到的中文对话已保留原文拼音和表情）

items.title: WarTalk: The View from AFRICOM
items.textEn: Africa is the literal center of the world’s map and increasingly the center of gravity for ISIS, the manpower source for Russia’s war in Ukraine, and the contested geopolitical ground where China builds bases and drops off free weapons. Our first active-duty guest pulls back the curtain on a combatant command that runs on 0.1% of the defense budget. LTG John W. Brennan Jr. is Deputy Commander of U.S. Africa Command and a 30-year career Special Forces officer, with command tours spanning 5th Special Forces Group, the anti-ISIS task force in Syria, and 1st Special Forces Command. He’s joined by ChinaTalk’s Justin, who served under Brennan as a young NCO in the Middle East. We discuss… How AFRICOM runs a counter-VEO away game on 0.1% of the defense budget by working "by, with, and through" partners "Putin’s Purse" : trafficking thousands of Africans onto the Ukrainian front lines under false pretenses The Houthi–al-Shabaab pipeline and the threat triangle around Djibouti’s PRC naval base Building an "alternate DIB in exile" : drone centers of excellence in Morocco, South African artillery, Namibian satellite radios Why Brennan wants to "declare jihad against proprietary data streams" and where AI actually helps a combatant commander decide Listen now on your favorite podcast app . Jordan Schneider: Our first active-duty guest here on WarTalk, joined today by Lieutenant General John W. Brennan Jr., Deputy Commander of U.S. Africa Command, dialing in from Stuttgart. He used to boss Justin around for a while, and we’re looking forward to Justin perhaps getting a little bit of revenge today. TBD. Justin: So I’ll start off with one of my favorite stories from my own career that doesn’t involve shooting or blowing things up. We had a major who I didn’t necessarily see eye to eye with. Colonel Brennan at the time had sent me to work in the Amman embassy, and this major decided that I needed to report to him instead of the Colonel. About two weeks went by, and all of a sudden I get an email: "Hey, haven’t heard much from you. What’s going on?" I forward him the reports that I’d been writing over that time. And then the next day Colonel Brennan comes over with an email very nicely to everyone that says, "Hey, just so everyone’s clear, Justin works for me and he reports only to me. So please direct all questions to the tower from this point forward." Really a storied career General Brennan has. I think letting him kick off, talking about his time — thirty-plus years in special operations, from Third Group, JSOC, Fifth Group commander — and then how you’ve seen the maturation of SOF and its role in the Army over that time, I think would be a really good way to kick us off, sir. John Brennan: Yeah, absolutely. Appreciate the intro, it was great letting you think I was in charge. While we were working this, the toughest problem set I think I had to work to this day, which was how to create a new army in Syria without talking to a Syrian or being in Syria. It started off really hard and it ended up finishing up in a very interesting way. But it shows you the power of SOF, and really the unconventional warfare aspect of what SOF and SF means, which is why we were created. So we’ve kind of come full circle through twenty-plus years of CT, but Syria is a great vignette of CT overlaid with unconventional warfare, because ISIS was the occupying power. We overthrew them. It took us a little longer than we wanted, but at the end of the day, that is why SF was made. Created by John F. Kennedy, going back to the Jedburghs — and we’re kind of full circle coming back to that, competing with China and Russia, enabling partners to overthrow an occupying power should that come to pass. So I think we’re setting the seeds for what needs to happen across the globe. It was great to come into the Army right on the heels of Desert Storm. Major conflict, but I watched everything transpire with defense cuts, and SOF actually grew during that interregnum period — late eighties, early nineties, we actually grew. In the nineties, the only people doing anything was SOF. SF in particular, I started off in Third Special Forces Group, probably deployed about two hundred and seventy days out of the year on the continent of Africa. When we weren’t doing Africa, we were also getting pulled into things like Bosnia, as well as Kuwait, because we had plans we finally executed in 2003 and overthrew Saddam Hussein. But it was the closest thing to a shooting war that was going on — the leftover residual conflict with the Iraqis, enforcing the no-fly zones, and working with indigenous folks actually in Iraq. Not a commonly known thing, that we had SOF teams on the ground in both northern and southern Iraq well into the nineties, before the war started, working with indigenous groups to keep tabs on what Saddam and his ilk were doing, and then set the conditions for an invasion if we needed to activate the then O-plan. Which actually was hugely beneficial — you saw it play out in 2003. You had Kurds rolling up the parachutes of the 173rd when they jumped in. For the price of a candy bar, you could get pulled out of the mud too. So, ten Special Forces guys with their partners on the battlefield. Again, the CT fight has shown over time the need for the interoperability of SOF and conventional forces, the power of combinations. We can’t do what we do globally without conventional support. We spot, assess, recruit, and train SOF operators from the conventional force for a reason. The lessons we learned over twenty-six-plus years of CT are still going on in Africa, by the way, and in the Middle East — we don’t want to forget them. But it’s been an interesting ride. Why Special Forces, and Why Now Justin: I really want to get to that. There’s been a lot of articles written in the last couple of weeks — Ned Marsh came out with a couple of pieces in Modern War Institute, Doc Duclos responded, there’s been some back and forth. What’s your take on the people calling for a wholesale personality change for special forces — that they need to come back and be reset — versus where you see the benefit being engagement, employment, and more contact? How does that balance look for you? John Brennan: Yeah, I think it’s all about working with indigenous folks, whether they’re existing military surrogates, to do the work in their country so we don’t have to send eighteen-year-old kids in tanks to some place to do it. The only people that can do that are special forces, SOF folks. And then we have, obviously, the high-end National Mission Force missions — but they recruit mostly out of existing SOF formations for a reason. It’s all about creative thinking, thinking outside the box, using old things in new ways and new things in new ways, to create effects wherever it’s needed. SF specializes in different parts of the world for a reason, so that you become experts and you have those relationships when you need them. Coming back here to AFRICOM in the twilight of my career has been interesting because the first place I went was Tunisia, and I met up with an old friend. We were friends as captains. He was in charge of their SOCOM. I’ve got examples of that in multiple countries. Those relationships matter, especially when you are in need and someone on the other end of that phone is a familiar voice. It really makes a huge difference. Justin: When you talk to policymakers or decision-makers, how do you bring that reality to them? Like, "Hey, I personally know this person, and this is what he’s telling me" — from a decades-old relationship that I’ve stayed in contact with. How does that translate when you talk to an NSC staffer or someone in a decision-making role? John Brennan: It’s hugely important. You can frame an action that may look one way, but you know the person — you’ve talked to them. You can tell a staffer or a decision-maker, "He’s doing this to get to this goal, and this is where we can help him. And if he goes across this line, he or she is probably not in line with our policy." Some of the coups that have happened in Africa — not every coup is a bloody one, and the reasoning behind it maybe is because the soldiers haven’t been getting paid. I’ve seen that happen. Going back to the nineties, I did a Flintlock-type event in Côte d’Ivoire and got home two weeks later, there was a coup. And I knew the guy on the front page of the Washington Post. He was hitting someone — he was actually hitting a rioter who wanted to take advantage of the coup. He was actually stopping it. His name was Sergeant First Class Sanson, and he hadn’t gotten paid, and his family hadn’t had the money to feed themselves until we showed up and they got what’s called 1206 monies from the embassy. They got extra stipends, because we train them hard, they need more calories. So I knew right away what he was doing and why. He was actually trying to stop violence, not create it. There are multiple examples of that I can point to. The Center of the World Justin: When you do have those engagements — one of the things we see is there’s a huge focus on Indo-Pacom, now there’s become a huge focus on Southern Command, and CENTCOM has always been the eight-hundred-pound gorilla. How do you talk about the importance of Africa and the role it plays in how you envision US national security and policy? John Brennan: Sure. You start by looking at a map. Africa’s the biggest landmass and it’s literally the center of the world. To get somewhere you have to go over it or around it. And six of the biggest, most important global chokepoints touch Africa. You see that play out in the policies of other countries. China tries to invest near the Strait of Gibraltar, near the Suez, near the Bab-el-Mandeb. Their only naval base outside of mainland China is in Djibouti, for a reason — because it’s the gateway to the …
items.summaryZh: 本文采访美国非洲司令部副司令约翰·布伦南将军，阐述非洲在全球地缘政治中的核心地位以及特种部队如何以极低的预算（仅占国防预算的0.1%）完成反恐、盟友培育和情报合作等任务，对投资者理解地区安全风险和供应链布局具有重要参考价值。
items.translationZh:
标题：WarTalk：来自非洲司令部的视角

正文：非洲是世界地图的字面中心，且日益成为ISIS的重心、俄罗斯在乌克兰战争的人力来源以及中国在此建立基地并投放免费武器的争夺战场。我们的第一位现役嘉宾将揭开一个仅占国防预算0.1%的作战指挥部的面纱。约翰·W·布伦南少将（LTG John W. Brennan Jr.）是美国非洲司令部副司令，拥有30年的特种作战经历，曾指挥第5特种部队、叙利亚的反ISIS特遣队以及第1特种部队司令部。他与ChinaTalk的贾斯汀（Justin）一起参与节目，贾斯汀曾在中东时期是布伦南的下属士官。我们讨论……

* AFRICOM如何在仅占国防预算0.1%的情况下，通过“与、通过、通过伙伴”来进行反VEO（暴力极端组织）对抗游戏
* “普京的钱包”：在虚假借口下将数千非洲人走私到乌克兰前线
* 也门胡塞‑索马里青年党（al‑Shabaab）管道以及围绕吉布提中国海军基地的威胁三角
* 建设“流亡的替代国防工业基地（DIB）”：摩洛哥的无人机卓越中心、南非的炮兵、纳米比亚的卫星收发站
* 布伦南为何想要“对专有数据流宣战”，以及人工智能在指挥官决策中真正发挥作用的地方

现在可在您喜欢的播客应用上收听。

乔丹·施耐德：我们的第一位现役嘉宾在WarTalk上，与约翰·W·布伦南少将（美国非洲司令部副司令）一起连线，布伦南正在斯图加特。曾经是贾斯汀的上司，今天期待贾斯汀或许可以报一小仇。待定。

贾斯汀：我先讲一个我职业生涯中最喜欢的、与射击或爆炸无关的故事。当时我们有一位上校不太跟我对付。布伦南上校把我派去安曼大使馆，而这位少校决定让我向他报告而不是向上校报告。大约两周后，我收到一封邮件：“嘿，好久没听你动静了，怎么了？”我把这段时间写的报告转发给他。第二天，布伦南上校发了一封非常客气的邮件给所有人：“大家请注意，贾斯汀归我指挥，只向我报告，请今后把所有问题发给指挥塔。”这位上校的职业生涯真是传奇。

约翰·布伦南：是的，当然。感谢介绍，让你们误以为我在掌舵。我们面对的最棘手的问题之一，就是如何在不与叙利亚人接触、也不进入叙利亚的情况下，创建一支新军队。起初非常困难，最后却以一种非常有趣的方式完成。这显示了特种部队的力量，以及非常规作战（unconventional warfare）在特种作战中的作用，这也是我们成立的原因。我们已经在二十多年反恐（CT）中走了一个完整的循环，而叙利亚是一个关于占领力量——ISIS——的非常规作战案例。我们推翻了他们，虽然用了比预期更长的时间，但这正是特种部队的初衷。特种部队由约翰·F·肯尼迪（John F. Kennedy）创建，可追溯到Jedburgh小组（二战期间的特种作战小组），我们正回到那个时代，与中国和俄罗斯竞争，帮助伙伴推翻占领力量（如果出现）。因此，我认为我们正在为全球需要发生的事情播下种子。我在沙漠风暴（Desert Storm）之后进入陆军，那时正是大型冲突时期，但我目睹了防务削减，特种部队在那段空档期（八九十年代初）仍然在增长。九十年代，唯一在行动的基本上是特种部队。尤其是特种部队，我在第三特种部队集团（Third Special Forces Group）开始服役，全年约有270天部署在非洲大陆。当我们不在非洲时，也会被派往波斯尼亚以及科威特，因为我们在2003年执行计划并推翻了萨达姆·侯赛因。但那是最接近真实战争的冲突——与伊拉克残余冲突，执行禁飞区，并与当地人共同作业。鲜为人知的是，我们在九十年代就已在伊拉克北部和南部有特种部队小组，与当地人一起监视萨达姆等人的动向，并为可能的行动（O‑plan）奠定条件。这在2003年的入侵中起了巨大作用。库尔德人在173空降师跳伞时帮助我们。只要付一根糖果棒的价钱，你就能从泥沼中被拉出来。于是，十名特种部队士兵与他们的伙伴一起上战场。再次说明，反恐作战长期显示出特种部队与常规部队的互操作性（interoperability）和组合力量（power of combinations）。没有常规部队的支持，我们无法在全球范围执行任务。我们挑选、评估、招聘并从常规部队中培养特种部队人员，这有其原因。我们在二十六年反恐经验中学到的教训仍在非洲和中东继续发挥作用，但我们不能忘记这些。真是一段有趣的旅程。

为何是特种部队，为何是现在？

贾斯汀：我真的想深入讨论。过去几周有很多文章——Ned Marsh在现代战争学院（Modern War Institute）发表了几篇文章，Doc Duclos作出回应，双方展开了争论。您如何看待那些呼吁彻底改变特种部队人格、进行重置的人——与您看到的参与、雇用以及更多接触的好处之间的平衡？

约翰·布伦南：我认为关键在于与当地人合作，无论他们是现有的军事代理，都在自己的国家完成任务，这样我们就不必让十八岁的孩子开坦克去执行。唯一能做到这一点的就是特种部队。我们还有高端国家任务部队（National Mission Force）的任务——但它们主要从现有特种部队编制中招募，原因显而易见。这全是创造性思维，跳出框架，旧东西用新方式，新东西用新方式，以在任何需要的地方产生效果。特种部队在世界不同地区专精，正因为如此，你会成为专家，在需要时拥有这些人脉。我的职业生涯后期回到AFRICOM非常有趣，因为我第一次去的是突尼斯，在那里与一位老朋友会面。我们当时是上尉，他负责他们的特种作战司令部（SOCOM）。我在多个国家都有类似的例子。这些关系很重要，尤其是当你需要帮助时，电话另一端的声音熟悉，它真的会产生巨大差异。

贾斯汀：当你向政策制定者或决策者传递这些现实时，如何表达？比如，“我个人认识这个人，他告诉我的情况”——这段多年建立的关系如何在向国家安全委员会（NSC）工作人员或其他决策者阐述时发挥作用？

约翰·布伦南：这极其重要。你可以框定一个行动，看起来可能是另一种形式，但你认识对方——你与他交谈过。你可以告诉工作人员或决策者：“他这样做是为了实现这个目标，我们可以在这里帮助他。如果他跨过这条线，他或她可能已经违背我们的政策。”非洲发生的政变并非每一次都是血腥的，背后的动因可能是士兵未领到工资。我曾在九十年代在科特迪瓦进行类似Flintlock的活动，两周后便发生了一场政变。我认识《华盛顿邮报》头版上那位军人。他当时在制止一名想利用政变的暴徒。他的名字是军士长桑森（Sergeant First Class Sanson），他因为未领到工资，家里也没钱吃饭，直到我们到达后他们才收到大使馆的1206款项（额外津贴），因为我们训练他们很严格，需要更多卡路里。所以我立刻明白他的行为动机——他是在阻止暴力，而不是制造暴力。我还能举出许多类似的例子。

世界的中心

贾斯汀：当你进行这些接触时，我们看到的一个现象是，印度‑太平洋司令部（Indo‑Pacom）受到极大关注，而现在南方司令部（Southern Command）也受到关注，中央司令部（CENTCOM）一直是“八百磅的大猩猩”。你如何谈论非洲的重要性以及它在你对美国国家安全和政策的设想中的角色？

约翰·布伦南：当然。先看一张地图。非洲是最大的陆块，实际上是世界的中心。要去任何地方，都必须经过或绕过它。六个最大、最重要的全球海上瓶颈都与非洲相连。你可以在其他国家的政策中看到这种布局。中国尝试在直布罗陀海峡、苏伊士运河以及巴布‑埃尔‑曼德海峡附近投资。他们在吉布提的唯一一个海外海军基地正是因为这里是通向…
